questions.questionText: Which of the following amino acids is required for purine ring synthesis?
questions.explanation: Gag: Glycine, Aspartate, Glutamine contribute atoms to the purine ring.

questions.questionText: The committed step in de novo purine biosynthesis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Transfer of the amide group from glutamine to PRPP is the committed step.

questions.questionText: The end product of purine catabolism in humans is:
questions.explanation: Xanthine oxidase converts hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid.

questions.questionText: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is due to a complete deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Defective purine salvage leads to excessive uric acid and self-mutilation.

questions.questionText: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) is classically caused by a deficiency of:
questions.explanation: ADA deficiency causes accumulation of dATP, which is toxic to lymphocytes.

questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme in de novo pyrimidine synthesis in mammals is:
questions.explanation: CPS II is cytosolic and synthesizes carbamoyl phosphate for pyrimidines.

questions.questionText: Methotrexate acts as an antineoplastic agent by competitively inhibiting:
questions.explanation: DHFR inhibition blocks the regeneration of tetrahydrofolate, halting thymidine synthesis.
5-FU acts as a suicide inhibitor of thymidylate synthase.

questions.questionText: Orotic aciduria (Type 1) is caused by a deficiency of the bifunctional enzyme UMP synthase. It presents with:
questions.explanation: Defective pyrimidine synthesis blocks cell division (anemia) and causes orotic acid in urine.

questions.questionText: Ribonucleotide reductase, the enzyme that converts ribose to deoxyribose, requires which protein cofactor?
questions.explanation: Thioredoxin serves as the hydrogen donor to reduce the ribose sugar.

questions.questionText: In Nucleotide Metabolism, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Biochemistry texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Biochemistry references for Nucleotide Metabolism. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Nucleotide Metabolism.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Nucleotide Metabolism scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Nucleotide Metabolism.
